That's just a shot in the dark, but let me know if it helps.

What version of ubuntu are you using and how do you have it set up in windows? You might want to try using docker. Someone on reddit said they were successfully using gdbgui with docker.

I see two reasons this could be happening:
1. gdbgui is sending some commands behind the scenes that are causing the error
2. gdbgui invokes rr with the "mi2" interpreter and exercises different code paths or rr by invoking mi commands 

To test the first:
* run gdbgui, open settings (gear icon in top right)
* check the box `Print all sent commands in console, including those sent automatically by gdbgui`
* re-run RR and see if anything looks suspicious. Copy a screenshot to here because if there is a bug that will definitely help me fix it.

It might also be that when gdbgui tries to insert a breakpoint at main automatically (can be configured in Settings > `Add breakpoint to main after loading executable`), that causes RR to segfault for your setup since maybe you don't have a `main` function?


If that doesn't yield any results, you can test the second theory. Try running rr from the terminal with `rr -i=mi2`, then running the same commands gdbgui runs and see if it segfaults.

I did some looking at the source to gevent to see how it is handling this.   So far as I can tell, it is *NOT* batching tasks around the event loop quite like this.   If I'm reading the source correctly (the `_run_callbacks()` method in https://github.com/gevent/gevent/blob/master/src/gevent/libev/corecffi.py), it looks like it runs all available callbacks (including new ones added by `run_callback()`), but with a counter.  After 1000 callbacks, it goes back to the event loop.

Ugh, and here's another nasty case that we should be aware of. With async generator support coming in, consider the effect of:

```python
async def ugh():
    async with curio.timeout_after(10):
        yield "have fun"

async def blargh():
    await ugh().__anext__()
    # ugh's timeout fires here, entirely outside of the with block
    await curio.sleep(20)
```

Probably there is no good solution right now except maybe to detect the worst cases and give some better-than-nothing error message. For the python 3.7 timeframe something like [PEP 521](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0521/) might be the solution...

I used to teach a concurrency course (primarily focused on threads).  In that class, I usually advised keeping the main-thread idle and running everything else in separate threads.   The main reason was to make it so that KeyboardInterrupt could continue to work.  However, that same advice applies to signals.   

I'd probably have to think about how this impacts Curio.   I haven't noticed an issue with signal delivery, but yes, if the main thread were getting blocked up for some reason, it might make more sense to run the Curio kernel in a separate thread.

Yeah, with that default formatter, the Quantity object is returning unicode from `__str__` (which print calls behind the scenes), which is bytes only in python2.

As a temporary fix, calling `format(c)`, or `print format(c)` will work, or defining a function like,

```python
def print_(x):
    print u'{}'.format(x)
```
On the pint end of it, to avoid this error, `Quantity.__str__` would have to return `str(format(self).encode('utf8'))`, rather than `format(self)` in python2.

I looked at the code and the problem is that you only link in the internal libraries. You don't link those in the `ext_libs` and `ext_vlibs` variables. The place you've included the internal libraries in the link command is after all object files, so as long as external libraries go after this then everything will work.

To explain the concept of secondary dependencies, let's say you have two libraries: `libalpha.so` and `libbeta.so`. The first of these depends on the second. Now say that you are writing a program which calls routines in `libalpha.so`, but does not (directly) call any routines in `libbeta.so`. (A real-world example of this would be programs calling LAPACK, which depends on BLAS.) Ideally, when linking your program, the only library you'd want to include in the link command is `libalpha.so`, as it is the only _direct_ dependency. However, the executable will still need to have `libbeta.so` available and it is a _secondary dependency_. 

The way that this can be achieved is to include `-lbeta` when linking the shared-object for `libalpha.so`. Then the only library which will need to be included when linking the program is `-lalpha`. Depending on where the libraries are located, it may be necessary to have some extra command specifying directories in which to look for them, but that's beyond the scope of this discussion.

That's very helpful @davidedelvento, I understand your use-case now. Agreed that there are two logically separate steps, "matching" and "diffing".  The changes you've already made have taken care of the "diffing" part, but for your use-case the "matching" step also needs updating, as you say.  Your proposal to just ignore columns with floating point values that are not equal may not quite work, since in the matching step we don't yet know about equality.  A revised proposal might be to ignore entire columns if they contain floating point values - that could work, but would leave nothing at all to match on in your case of a table that is all floats.

Here's an idea. Suppose we defined two parameters: 10^-m and 10^-n, where m<n.  When working on matching rows, up until now each row in a table has mapped to a single hash entry, which we can then find quickly in a second table (I know this seems overkill for your case, but it is needed in the general case of insertion/deletion/reordering). Suppose we now relax the constraint, and let a row map to multiple hash entries.  In particular, for a cell containing a float, we will add and subtract 10^-n to it, and then round the two extremes to the nearest 10^-m.  The rounded values may be identical or different. If identical, hashing proceeds exactly as before.  If different, we need to produce two hashes for that row.  Repeating this process across multiple float cells, we may need to double the number of rows multiple times (the odds of this happening depend on choice of m and n).

Matching this way, we can guarantee that cells that differ less than 10^-n will be treated as the same and matched.  The downside is that cells that differ more than 10^-n may well also be treated as the same, since we round off to 10^-m.  But, with a tweak to offset the 10^-m rounding away from aligning with integer boundaries, I think this might prove a scalable solution in practice. 

Backing out of the details - how would you feel about a matching mechanism that guaranteed that:
 * numbers within 0.0001 of each other would be treated as identical
 * numbers differing by more than 0.1 would be treated as distinct
and made no promises about what happened in between?

My best guess is that what's happening here is that the data marshalling
code is sending something to the DBus daemon that isn't formatted correctly
and that's causing it to silently drop the connection. As to why the

The tcpdump output here looks exactly correct to me. Where did the 66ms come from?

```python
@sd.timer('wait_1')
@sd.timer('wait_2')

def wait_1():
        time.sleep(1)
```

This decorates the function `wait_1` with two timers. The "inner" one is `test.wait_2`, so it completes first, then `test.wait_1` finishes. (That's why `test.wait_2` is 1001ms and `test.wait_1` is 1002ms.)

The function `wait_2` sleeps for 10s, and is not decorated at all, so

```python
wait_2()
wait_1()
```

causes the script to sleep for 10s, reporting nothing, then sleep for 1s and report two timers. Then finally

```python
with sd.timer('test2'):
        wait_2()
```

sleeps for 10 seconds within a timed block, and submits `test.test2` with a value of ~10s.

If you want to decorate the `wait_2` function, you'd need to do something like this:

```python
@sd.timer('wait_1')
def wait_1():
    time.sleep(1)

@sd.timer('wait_2')
def wait_2():
    time.sleep(10)
```

To rephrase:

1) The `None` vs `''` content/type problem in #24 is a matter of distinguishing between misconfiguration and deliberate circumvention, and requires always remembering to pass in `''` for empty content/type (a subtlety easily forgotten or misunderstood).

2) The `accept_untrusted_content` option only applies when a hash is omitted, so the shortcut of
```python
Receiver(lookup_hawk_credentials,
         auth_header,
         url,
         env.get('REQUEST_METHOD'),
         accept_untrusted_content=True)
```
fails when the client *does* supply a hash, negating the verbosity argument. Basically, one always has to pass `content`, `content_type`, and `accept_untrusted_content` to cover all cases. (Perhaps calling it `allow_omitted_hash` would be more accurate.)

3) There is in fact a security difference between allowing an unhashed, *empty* content/type and not checking the hash when provided even with empty content/type. If a hash is supplied, it *should* be checked without fail (and `None` vs `''` shouldn't make a difference). But where empty content/type is expected, it should be easy to allow, and right now one basically has to read through `HawkAuthority._authorize()` and `Resource.get_content_hash()` to understand when and how the parameters interact.

Yes, this is intended for all OSes, even though Windows is probably gonna be the most difficult platform because it has less C APIs which can be used to retrieve multiple info in one shot.
For instance, BSD is the exact opposite, as in one shot you get a whole blob of stuff:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/50015c4073f741e89e29f67b74bded47a25a3a49/psutil/_psutil_bsd.c#L234
The only Windows C call I can think of that is being used basically all the time on Windows is `OpenProcess`.
We use a wrapper around it:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/50015c4073f741e89e29f67b74bded47a25a3a49/psutil/arch/windows/process_info.c#L21
...which is extensively used in the main C extension module:

```
grep psutil_handle  psutil/_psutil_windows.c | wc -l
16
```

What we can do is get the handle once, store it in Python (as an `int`), then pass it back to the C extension as an argument, and do this as long as we're in the `oneshot` context (then on `__exit__` we're gonna "`CloseHandle()` it"). The methods involved should be (at least): `cpu_times()`, `create_time()`, `memory_info()`, `nice()`, `io_counters()`, `cpu_affinity()`, `num_handles()` and `memory_maps()`. So yes, also on Windows there's a lot of space for speeding things up quite a bit.

So, now, since the original request no longer reflects the reality, can I ask you to remove this feature?

The motivation is similar to what I wrote earlier: there is no plausible explanation for why a client should stop receiving messages. Any situation when that happens is an error.  Don't misunderstand me: users of the client may chose to stop receiving messages (simply by breaking out of the loop), but the client itself should not make such decisions on their behalf.

Think about it the same way as you would think about operations on files: from filesystem, or from kernel's perspective, there is no plausible explanation for why would you like `read()` operation to succeed, when the file was not found.  Users of `read()`, however, may chose to ignore this error. It would be a really bad idea if `read()` was configurable to sometimes silently ignore errors.  History, actually knows instance of when this did happen: it was possible once to mount NFS shares specifying "soft" as mount mode: in this situation, a share could be unmounted while I/O is still in progress.  Eventually, NFS Linux client developers realized how bad this idea was, and removed this feature altogether, without replacement.

> Well, it seems that xclip is kind of broken in general when used in pipes.

This appears to be the documented behavior of its default `-silent` (i.e. daemon) mode:

> fork into the background to wait for requests, no informational output, errors only (default)
>
> The default action is to silently wait in the background for X selection requests (pastes) until another X application places data in the clipboard, at which point xclip exits silently.

```sh
echo "foo" | xclip -selection clipboard | cat
<hangs>
```
```sh
echo "foo" | xclip -selection clipboard -silent | cat
<hangs>
```

It doesn't hang for me (xclip 0.13 on Linux (Arch)) if it's instructed to work as a filter (i.e. kept in the foreground) via the `-f`/`-filter` option e.g.:

```sh
echo "foo" | xclip -selection clipboard -f | cat
foo
```

Indeed it seems that floating point accuracy plays a role here.

The best option to add the `abs`, to keep the original behavior is to add a parameter to the indicator. This is the same that was done with the Stochastic, for people that had extreme conditions and hit the undetermined division.

Something like:

```python
class StandardDeviation(Indicator):
    params = (('period', 20), ('movav', MovAv.Simple), ('_abs', False),)

    def __init__(self):
        ...
        if self.p._abs:
            self.lines.stddev = pow(abs(meansq - sqmean), 0.5)
        else:
            self.lines.stddev = pow(meansq - sqmean, 0.5)
```

You simply pass `_abs=True` when creating the indicator if you suspect your values are going to hit the precision limits.

Python:

```
def setTimeout(func, delay):
    func()

ctx = js2py.EvalJs({'setTimeout': setTimeout})
# afterwards you can simply use
ctx.eval("setTimeout(function k() {console.log('timeout reached!')})")
```

JavaScript - you can simply prepend your Js code with a definition of setTiemout. 

`js_code = 'function setTimeout(func, delay) {func()}' + js_code`

With FORCE_PROXY and clearnet ports completely disabled Electrum treats Tor as a standard socks5 proxy and connects to other peers (both .onion and clearnet) through it and since it does so, it also servers a list of peers obtained this way to clients. The idea with onlynet=onion is to keep all comms Tor-only.

My config (relevant variables)
PEER_ANNOUNCE on
PEER_DISCOVERY on
REPORT_HOST <unset>
REPORT_SSL_PORT 0
REPORT_TCP_PORT 0
REPORT_HOST_TOR <set to an onion address>
REPORT_SSL_PORT_TOR  50002
REPORT_TCP_PORT_TOR  50001
SSL_PORT 50002
TCP_PORT 50001
FORCE_PROXY on

The point is to allow only Tor comms, i.e. connecting and announcing only to other Tor-only servers and accepting Tor-only clients and serving them only other Tor-only peers.

Hi @jakobandersen, thank you very much for your continued work on both supporting C++ in Sphinx, and helping Breathe catch up!

**TL;DR**: the main tension is that currently breathe injects a bunch of nodes when rendering things, but what we need to do is just create the nodes and populate the correct member variables and appending children in the graph (AKA it might be that we just remove our sphinx renderer altogether?)?

I just did a little more digging to test this, I made a really quick and dirty check repository: https://github.com/svenevs/toward_templates

All I did was use the Breathe code for the directive, but not incorporate anything from the sphinx renderer in breathe.

1. [This class definition](https://github.com/svenevs/toward_templates/blob/c26fcc8b91afa30bffd5281476131a6751e3044b/_extensions/template_test.py#L4) is just copied from Breathe.
2. I just add a simple directive in `conf.py:setup(app)` called `template-test`.
3. I invoke it once in `index.rst` with

    ```rst
    .. template-test:: template< int N > Foo

       Foo takes in a template argument.
    ```

This builds exactly as expected.  So I think that means that for Breathe, we just need to:

1. Remove the injection of an extra node for templates on classes etc.
2. Figure out how to use `tparam` with these classes at creation of the node (for documentation coming from `\tparam` in Doxygen).
    - So we need to figure out how to populate this thing at the creation of the node, rather than during rendering of the object.
3. When specifying say member attributes or member functions, modify the signature being created.
    - This is what you are describing [in the 284 comment](https://github.com/michaeljones/breathe/issues/284#issuecomment-395982957), but my understanding is this is currently not possible *at the top-level*.
    - So what we currently are doing (I think) is trying to add everything to the `CPPClassObject` (incorrect).
    - What we need to be doing is adding children.  So the equivalent of this:

        ```rst
        .. template-test:: template< int N > Foo

           Foo takes in a template argument.

           .. cpp:member:: static constexpr int n

              Convenience alias to template parameter ``N``.
        ```

Does this sound correct?

I fixed it by initializing all the style specifiers to `None` in `apply_headers_style`.

This way we can check if any of them is not `None` (then displaying the deprecation warning). Only then we set the default style.

```python
def apply_headers_style(self, styler_obj=None, bg_color=None, bold=None, font=None, font_size=None,
                        font_color=None, protection=None, number_format=None,
                        underline=None):

.
.
.
     elif any((bg_color, bold, font, font_size, font_color, protection, number_format, underline)):
         warnings.warn(DEPRECATION_MSG, DeprecationWarning)

     # applying "default" header style. TODO: Delete when removing support for directly passing
     # style specifiers
     # see issue #24: https://github.com/DeepSpace2/StyleFrame/issues/24
     bg_color = bg_color or utils.colors.white
     bold = bold or True
     font = font or "Arial"
     font_size = font_size or 12
     font_color = font_color or utils.colors.black
     protection = protection or False
     number_format = number_format or utils.number_formats.general
     underline = underline or None
```

I've analysed the binary data encoded in the base64 strings provided by @cnrd and I've reverse-engineered  the format of the signal. It's really simple and won't be able to properly learn some IR protocols. I won't go into details here as the IR control subject is really big and ugly.
I was able to extract the signal from the base64 for prosonic power button (it's NEC1, Dev=0, Func=10).
The format stores only a single stream of pulses and gaps. It uses little endian.
$00 - 2 bytes - modulation frequency given as off and on time (one byte each), the unit is [100 nanoseconds]
$02 - 2 bytes - number of rising/falling edges + 1
$04 - 16 x 4 bytes - index of 16 4-byte integers representing possible time values in milliseconds, sorted by ascending value - index 0 is at $04, index 1 is at $08 etc.
$44 - start of IR stream - its length is the value from $04 * 4bits, rounded up to a whole byte

Each byte in IR stream represents a pair of a pulse and gap. The least significant 4 bits contain an index position of time of the pulse, followed by a gap which time is contained under index value represented by most significant 4 bits.

Having that information, it should not be hard to write method in the ChuangmiIr class, which would take other signal formats (like the most popular Pronto Hex), convert them on the fly and send them out.

My test on the face detection was pretty good. A small test (less than 100 images) shows roughly 97% accuracy. A large test (10K images) shows roughly 91% of accuracy. A nice thing about the face detection here is it can miss faces, but when it detects a face, it is truly the face.

However, face recognition outcome is not acceptable at all. Firstly trying looking for a good tolerance is almost impossible. When I tried to recognize a person among 3000 candidates, a tolerance of 0.6 can output more than 20 people, while 0.4 outputs none, and 0.45 outputs 2 to 3. When I change to test with another image, the tolerance has to be changed. Then I tried to output the minimum distance. It works for some. With 3000 possible candidates, and most of them with 1 images, and all of the 233 test images are the people whose encodings are in the list, only 61 were correctly recognized (and 18 of them cannot produce encodings). When I increase the candidates images to about 4 to 6, it becomes 76 out of 233, which means the accuracy is only 32%. I had a small set, with about 2K candidates, the accuracy is about 45% when each person is roughly 1 images.

Sorry I never saw this issue. The code isn't doing what it "says" its doing, its using a weird algorithm to get an id for the occurrences in fullcalendar (NOT THE SAME AS IN THE DB) which are always unique.

The issue arises because in general you want moving an event to move every unpersisted occurrence of that event (like editing the event), so you need to give all of the unpersisted occurrences the same id so they move together, but those id's can't interfere with the id's of the persisted occurrences, so I give them all the same id = (largest id of any persisted occurrence in the database ) + 1 + event_id, making sure each group is unique, but the same within the group.

I would say there is no clear advantage, except maybe being more intuitive to the user (but that might be just me). Having a `harvester.device_info_list` with multiple cameras makes you think that you could also connect multiple cameras with that HC object, but of course this is impossible with the current implementation.

An alternative implementation would need a separate class for `Device`, which could be used like 
```
camera1 = harvester.get_device(0)  # Returns a Device object
camera2 = harvester.get_device(1)
```
or something like that. So the `Device` class would have many of the features that `Harvester` class currently has. Then the device objects could be used separately:
```
camera1.start_image_acquisition()
camera2.start_image_acquisition()

# Acquisition of images with the cameras
...

camera1.stop_image_acquisition()
camera2.stop_image_acquisition()

```

I think this way the process of connecting and disconnecting of the device could be made automatic, like for example connecting the device in the `__init__` of the `Device` and disconnecting it in the `__del__`. If there is a separate `Device` class, then the role of `Harvester` object would be handling the cti-files and listing the available cameras.

However, I'm not sure if this is any better or more difficult from the maintenance point of view. I want to add that I'm totally happy with the current implementation of Harvester, since it works just fine. :)

I just recalled that I forgot to add `CollationHeader.number` field in doc.md last year. Let's review again in the current paradigm:

Currently, the sequential number of collation is exactly the score of collation.

Should we add `number` field in `CollationHeader`?

The following evaluation is made in September last year with `pyethereum` structure.

Cons
1. Taking more space for storing this one new field.
2. The cost of switching shard branch would increase by additional data management:
if we want to use a function like `ShardChain.get_collation_by_number(collation_number)`, it may takes more computational time upon receiving a new block and reorganization the collation head since we have to reorganize database values like: `self.db.put(b'coll:%d' % collation.header.number, collation.header.hash)`
    * ^^^^^ Won't be an issue for stateless client shuffling.

Pros
1. It’s easier to delete old junk collations in database with `CollationHeader.number` since it’s easier to locate the old collation by their number.
2. It’s easier to peek the score of an orphan collation with `CollationHeader.number`:
it would help the shard validators to determine if they fell behind when the given collation’s parent is not in the database, and then they can ask for the parent collations. Otherwise, the shard validators have to call validator manager contract to check the score after they received the block which includes the given collation.

Look over again, I think it's still worth to add `number` field for archival nodes.

@FabrizioA @pinksharpii 

Hey,
i asked @czpython for help and he told me this:


There are different ways to solve this:

One is to override the article detail template and add a static placeholder there.
The declaration of the static placeholder needs to contain something unique to the article, like so:

{% static_placeholder 'article_image_'|add:article.pk %}

This would cause a static placeholder to be created for each article.

Personally, I don't like this approach because it makes use of a technicality within the cms and as such might break in the future.
Plus it binds the data to the template instead of the article itself.

The recommended approach is to create an app in your project with a model that has a one to one relationship to the article
and a placeholder field for the image or other content:

Class ArticleExtension(models.Model):
    article = models.OneToOneField(Article, related_name='extension')
    image_placeholder = PlaceholderField('article_image')

Then in your template you can do something like:

{% if article.extension %}
    {% render_placeholder article.extension.image_placeholder %}
{% endif %}

Its a bit more work than the template based approach but in my opinion is cleaner and more explicit.


Maybe it will helps you.

Greetings Danny

I'm working on support for upgrade series, and this seems like a good time to resolve this as well as all of the other hook-informed flags.  We need to sort out the naming and how to transition from the current behavior for flags that are currently managed by layers, like `config.` and `leadership.`.

I've been leaning toward having prefixes for flag names to make them more explicit and avoid conflicts.  The `endpoint.` prefix seemed to help quite a bit with confusion when a charm and endpoint shared the same or even just a similar name.  We also don't want to end up "reserving" too many flag names that the charm author would want to use.  On the other hand, prefixes do make flag names longer, and we do have the existing flags that don't have a common prefix.  (I've also started using the convention in my charms of using the `charm.<charm-name>.` prefix for flags for the charm layer and `layer.<layer-name>.` in base layers.)

For the existing `config.` flags, I think we decided that it would cause issues if we got rid of the logic that removes the flags at the end of the hook, but didn't we have an option for transitioning to that behavior?  Something like a layer option?

I agree that agents should respect action ranges, and that baselines does a bad job of this right now. We not only don't clip actions, we also don't stretch the policy outputs to the correct range. For example, if an action space has a large range like [-100, 100], our agents would still start by outputting values with stddev 1.

The question isn't really whether this is a problem--it's how to best fix the problem. I proposed doing the fix in a wrapper (which could be placed in `baselines/common`). This seems like a hack, and it kind of is. However, it would be easy to drop the wrapper into every algorithm in baselines and have an immediate universal fix. All the other fixes I can think of would be algorithm-specific.

@maximecb what do you have in mind when you say the model should output values in the correct range? If you parameterize a policy as a Gaussian, it *must* be able to output values in [-inf, inf]. If you clip the outputs coming out of the policy and use these clipped values in a policy gradient update, the resulting gradient will be biased (since the log probs won't reflect the clipped pdf). No matter how you slice it, the policy must *believe* that it can take any action value it wants to, otherwise the policy gradient is wrong.

There are plenty of implementation-specific points to insert clipping (e.g. the argument to step() [in PPO1](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/98257ef8c9bd23a24a330731ae54ed086d9ce4a7/baselines/ppo1/pposgd_simple.py#L51)). However, using a wrapper will not require changing every single implementation, while pretty much any other approach will.

As a side note, the Gaussian distribution is probably not ideal for these kinds of problems anyway. See, for example, this paper on using the [Beta distribution](https://www.ri.cmu.edu/wp-content/uploads/2017/06/thesis-Chou.pdf) in RL. The Beta distribution is bounded between 0 and 1, making it more appropriate for problems where the action space is constrained to an interval.

We eventually got around this by switching from buildpacks to using Dockerfiles for building and deploying. In essence we pull in our custom Docker image, make sure cURL is installed along with a few other things, then pull the private repo key from a private S3 bucket (doesn't have to be S3, could be any location), the make sure that is in the correct location for the build process. Here is a sanitised version of the Dockerfile I am now using...

	# Set the base image to Debian Jessie with Node.js
	FROM crispy1975/jessie-nodejs:latest

	# File Author / Maintainer
	MAINTAINER Crispy1975

	# Install Node.js and other dependencies
	RUN apt-get update \
		&& apt-get -y install git-core \
		&& apt-get -y install curl \
		&& apt-get -y install openssh-client \
		&& apt-get -y install python \
		&& apt-get -y install build-essential \
		&& apt-get -y upgrade

	# Copy repo access keys over
	RUN mkdir -p /root/.ssh \
		&& curl -SLO https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/my-bucket/my-key \
		&& mv my-key /root/.ssh/id_rsa \
		&& chmod 600 /root/.ssh/id_rsa \
		&& ssh-keyscan github.com >> /root/.ssh/known_hosts

	# Provide a cached layer for node_modules
	COPY package.json /tmp/package.json
	RUN cd /tmp && npm install
	RUN mkdir -p /src && cp -a /tmp/node_modules /src/

	# Remove key for security
	RUN rm -R /root/.ssh

	# Define working directory
	WORKDIR /src
	COPY . /src

	# Move into the src folder
	RUN cd /src

	# Expose port (override this elsewhere)
	EXPOSE  80

	# Run the app
	ENTRYPOINT ["npm", "start"]

Hope that helps somebody having problems with this. Would also love to hear if there is a better way.

An investigation has been performed.

`DataSet.get_data` calls `get_data` function from `sqlite_base`. `get_data` queries the data, and then calls `many_many` to "fetch" it and reshape list of rows into list of columns:
```python
res = curr.fetchall()
results = []
for r in res:
    results.append([r[c] for c in columns])
return results
```
It turns out that profiling shows that `many_many` takes 99% of the time of the `DataSet.get_data` call, and ~12% of that time is spent on the list comprehension, and ~40% of the time is spend in `_convert_numeric` function (that converts sqlite type into python floats or integers).

Using cursor `curr` as iterator instead of `curr.fetchall()` does not provide any improvement.

Using `DataSet.get_data` for multiple parameter names (columns names) at once, instead of calling `get_data` for each parameter separately, is faster. For example, calling `get_data` once with 4 parameter names as its arguments is ~40% faster than calling `get_data` 4 times for 4 parameters.

I could not find any way for `sqlite3` to return already columns of data instead of rows (yes, its highly efficient `sqlite3.Row`). Hence, reshaping the "list of `sqlite3.Row`s" seems unavoidable.

Note, I'm not a Let's Encrypt developer.

The rationale for Let's Encrypts short certificate lifetime is [here](https://letsencrypt.org/2015/11/09/why-90-days.html). The aim is to encourage automation by making it relatively burdensome for a sysadmin to have to update certificates manually. You're suggesting you want security using SSL, HSTS preloading HPKP certificate pinning and DANE, but with a sysadmin who will update all these things manually rather than scripting them. It's precisely this which leads to websites with invalid certificate configurations which users then have to ignore to access.

SSL Labs doesn't require HSTS preloading to get an A+ score. It requires that the server supplies a max-age value of at least six months in the Strict-Transport-Security header. Doing this does not require a certificate lifetime of more than 3 months, neither does adding your site to the HSTS preload list.

RFC7469 suggests a max-age value of 2 months. It says nothing about what the lifetime of a useful certificate should be. You don't need to pin the leaf certificate, it's possible to pin using any certificate in the trust chain including the Let's Encrypt CA's, which is then independent of the lifetime of your certificate. This protects you from a compromise of any CA except Let's Encrypt. Let's say you don't trust Let's Encrypt not to be compromised though. HPKP is based on the *public key* of the certificate used. This is generated by the *client*, and can therefore remain constant for longer than the individual certificate lifetime as long as you re-use the same CSR (which you can do with Let's Encrypt).  

With DANE it's also possible to specify that certificates should only be accepted when issued by a certain CA, which is again independent of certificate lifetime. If you do want to tie TLSA records directly to your leaf certificates, as before, you can ensure they have the same public key. If they don't, it's easily scriptable to manage the DNS updates if you run your own DNS server or are using a cloud DNS solution which provides its own API.

> I think it was a security issue to allow read by default.

I'm not questioning the intent of the original issue. Just pointing out that the implementation as merged is breaking people's existing code & assumptions without saying so and with no efficient option to revert. If you look at my proposed solution again, what I'm advocating is to give people options with a safe and backwards compatible default.

> I think breaking backward-compat is necessary in this context.

I disagree. The change as currently merged not only breaks backwards-compatibility but also introduces a much larger potential security issue in that the obvious way (implied by the current implementation) is to assign the change permission to all users that should be allowed GET. 

Frankly, I fail to see how the `change` permission for read actions increases security as at the same time this permission also allows PUT. Mixing permissions for different actions doesn't seem a good choice.

Unfortunately, your proposed `ModifiedDjangoAuthorization` won't do the trick unless you actually add the `view` permission to the model, so it is again breaking backwards compatibility. At least it requires changing code -- so we break backwards compatibility _and_ force users to rework their code base. 

Of course overriding is always an option to achieve one's specific requirements, however I think the general idea in tastypie is to provide sensible & safe defaults that don't require adding custom code...

In short I think this change should be reverted for a better implementation.

I'm not quite sure that's basically the same thing. Disabling the SSL cert validation opens you up to a MITM attack that you won't be able to detect, but at least to the general bystander your traffic is encrypted. It would require a rather sophisticated attack to take advantage of. The same can not be said for running without encryption at all where anybody with a packet sniffer can see your data.

The proper way to fix this is with implementing SSL certificate pinning, either by shipping your public Root CA chain or pinning to your issued certificate directly.

I've been using black only for a couple of weeks. From my limited experience I have the say that I am beginning to like it. At first I was also startled by some of its decisions, but gradually I've become accustomed to it and I like that I don't have to think about style anymore. Well, unless when sometimes it looks too odd, in which case instead of `fmt:off` I tend to change variable names, or put some parentheses, so the line breaks where I want.
I tried other autoformatters like yapf, but unlike black these have a huge list of options and configuration. Paradoxically, I actually like that black does _not_ allow me to change things! Perhaps it's me that I'm lazy.
And of course, there's the thing that you don't have to argue with other contributors and maintainers about formatting of PRs. You can just tell them to run black (or tox -e format, or whatever) or even set up a pre-commit hook if they wish.
Anyway, I understand that for a "mature" project like dateutil it's not easy to make the switch. Even pip considered using black but then decided to not do that, as some maintainers didn't like all of blacks style rules, or wished to have more flexibility or control over formatting.

@tim-one I tend to agree with that assessment. The one thing I'll mention is that it might be a good idea for me to rename the `_fold` flag here, because it's not actually intended to be an indicator of whether or not the datetime _is_ in a fold, rather it's supposed to be the _default value_ for `fold` for a given ambiguous date. In this setup, `_fold` is intended to be meaningless except when handling ambiguous dates.

This allows you to do things like this:

```python
from dateutil import tz
from dateutil.rrule import rrule, SU, YEARLY

from datetime import datetime

NYC = tz.gettz('America/New_York')

# 1:30 AM during a transition on the EDT side
dst_edt = datetime(2015, 11, 1, 5, 30, tzinfo=tz.tzutc()).astimezone(NYC)

# 1:30 AM during a transition on the EST side
dst_est = datetime(2015, 11, 1, 6, 30, tzinfo=tz.tzutc()).astimezone(NYC)

# 1st Sunday in November every year (DST rule)
rrule_kwargs = dict(freq=YEARLY, bymonth=11, byweekday=SU(+1), count=3)

dst_rrule_edt = rrule(dtstart=dst_edt, **rrule_kwargs)
dst_rrule_est = rrule(dtstart=dst_est, **rrule_kwargs)

strfmt = '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S %Z%z'
for d_edt, d_est in zip(dst_rrule_edt, dst_rrule_est):
    print("EDT: {}".format(d_edt.strftime(strfmt)))
    print("EST: {}".format(d_est.strftime(strfmt)))
```

Result:

```
EDT: 2015-11-01 01:30:00 EDT-0400
EST: 2015-11-01 01:30:00 EST-0500
EDT: 2016-11-06 01:30:00 EDT-0400
EST: 2016-11-06 01:30:00 EST-0500
EDT: 2017-11-05 01:30:00 EDT-0400
EST: 2017-11-05 01:30:00 EST-0500
```

Without having to worry about whether the `rrule` calculation passed through some intermediate stage. Again, without a hook in to the `tzinfo` object during arithmetic operations, I don't think there's any good way to cover all cases.

Question, after PEP 495 is implemented, if I do something like this:

```
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from dateutil import tz

NYC = tz.gettz('America/New_York')

before_dst = datetime(2015, 11, 1, 0, 30, tzinfo=NYC)
in_transition = before_dst + timedelta(hours=1)
```

Will the arithmetic operation correctly set `in_transition.fold` to 1?

So there is no parameter for custom headers in boto3's generate_presigned_url. The headers get added based on the parameters that you would normally pass to the client's method. So something like this:
```py
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

# Generate the URL to get 'key-name' from 'bucket-name'
url = s3.generate_presigned_url(
    ClientMethod='put_object',
    Params={
        'Bucket': 'bucket-name',
        'Key': 'key-name',
        'ContentType': 'image/jpeg'
    }
)
print(url)
```

@fhieber I have merged the PR https://github.com/awslabs/mxboard/pull/15 to resolve the naming conflict of embedding data.
The rule is:
If `global_step=None`, the folder name is just `tag`.
Else, the folder name is `tag + '_' + str(global_step).zfill(6)`.
See doc here: https://github.com/awslabs/mxboard/blob/master/python/mxboard/writer.py#L389

You just need to either keep `tag` or combination of `tag` and `global_step` unique throughout training to prevent overwriting embedding data. Let me know if it serves what you need.

Just to avoid confusion: I assume you want to run on multiple CPU-cores, not on physically different CPU-devices/chips. Correct?
If above is correct, and then the system will use automatically multiple cores. MXNet as the underlying DL-framework uses OpenMP as the parallelization framework, so the number of threads can be controlled by usual OpenMP-environment variables e.g.  OMP_NUM_THREADS.
Though this should be unnecessary as the system will automatically figure that out internally. 

If you see something like 200% CPU usage in "top" when running on a 20-core machine, then certainly something is not working as expected. It looks as if the BLAS library that is part of the installation works just single threaded (the bulk of the computation in Sockeye is matrix-matrix multiplies and the key to speedup is to parallelize internally each matrix-matrix multiply).
 
My first advice would be to install the MKL version in a clean separate conda environment and give it a try.

I had some time now to think about how to possibly use Z3's command `check-with-assumptions`.

Below is the best algorithm I have found so far. pseudo-code is executed externally to the solver, whereas Lisp-style code is submitted to the solver.

```
externally define the constraint sets Ci comprising domain constraints cij
form a set C containing all constraint sets Ci

;; solve the background CSP y
(assert y)  ; enter y
(check-sat) ; compute a base solution as a starting point for further ado

set round counter k to 1
while C is non-empty
  ;; start new round k trying to produce a solution Mk
  (declare-const pik Bool) ; for current round k and all i with non-empty Ci in C
  (assert (=> pik (or ci1 ci2 ...))) ; for current round k and all i with non-empty Ci in C
  (check-with-assumptions p1k p2k ...)
  if sat
    (get-model)
    save solution Mk
    analyze solution and remove satisfied constraints cik from the sets Ci
    restore full set C with all remaining non-empty constraint sets Ci
    increment k
  else
    if possible minimize the unsat core
    analyze the unsat-core and find conflicting variables pik (k is fixed, i varies)
    temporarily remove conflicting Ci's from C, there by reducing the number of active pik
  endif
endwhile
```

If an attempt to find a solution fails in round k, it should succeed in the next round k+1, since we have removed the conflicts.

I do not know whether I have fully grasped your proposal on how to use check-with-assumptions, 
but to me it looks as if the algorithm above should work. Moreover, I believe it would be faster
than trying to truly optimize the number of domain constraints covered in each Mk. 
This believe stems from our experience (with Yices 1) that conflicts are detected much faster 
than what it takes to produce a solution

The proof that `vd=nNsVth*log(IL/I0 + 1)` corresponds to a voltage greater than voc is here:
![vocest-proof](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1385621/35656666-5f59cd9a-06ad-11e8-8aa2-8e8eb9f7c603.png)

Since the current is negative, it _must_ be in the 4th quadrant, which means that the voltage _must_ be greater than voc. **QED** So you can see that if you use this estimate for the diode voltage and a zero diode voltage, then you have completely bounded the iv curve and therefore you can find any point inside it using bisection. This is foolproof. I hope I don't regret saying that.

Regarding pytest, that sounds good for many of the tests.  eigen.py/cpp (which you converted) comes to mind: it mostly consists of output of a bunch of lines such as "test_whatever() OK" or "test_whatever() FAILED"--which is basically already just a test script, but with less sophistication than pytest would give us.  On the other hand, for many of the example scripts in example/*.py the python code is, conceptually, part of the example, where providing the python-side counterpart to something in pybind11 is useful. I recently learned that the VSTS team is using Chocolatey to help create the baseline images that are used for their build agents (I stumbled across this information via a Stack Overflow question [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/49294430/how-to-run-or-install-tool-on-hosted-agent-in-vsts/49317494#49317494) , and after talking with the team, the [documentation](https://github.com/Microsoft/vsts-image-generation/blob/master/images/win/Vs2017-Server2016-Readme.md#chocolatey) was updated to reflect that Chocolatey is available already installed on the build agents).

This failed for a different reason, but for @devigned here's what did work (thanks to @derekbekoe):

After you install the pre-reqs, run:
`wget https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py`
`sudo python get-pip.py`

This will install the latest version of PIP, and then your pip install should succeed. If you do what Ubuntu suggest and do `sudo apt-get install python-pip` you will get an ancient version of pip that will not work.

	def create_network_interface(self,network_client, region, group_name, interface_name,network_name, subnet_name, ip_name):
	
		result = self.network_client.virtual_networks.create_or_update(
			group_name,
			network_name,
			azure.mgmt.network.models.VirtualNetwork(
				location=region,
				address_space=azure.mgmt.network.models.AddressSpace(

				],
			),
		)
		result.wait()
		subnet = self.network_client.subnets.get(group_name, network_name, subnet_name)
	
		result = self.network_client.public_ip_addresses.create_or_update(

I will give a precis here, but this should be described in full in the docs.

Every up/download saves itself on each change in state (i.e., any chunk completed/errored). They will remain amongst the list of persisted transfers until they are completed, unless the user explicitly calls clear. Any future session (or later in the same session) can resume a transfer, and all chunks that were not already successfully moved will get restarted, until done.

In the CLI, this manifests as list_uploads/list_downloads, which will give a text list of hash keys for saved transfers, and brief status that was last recorded. We trust that the state of the source and destination hasn't changed (i.e., this is not meant to be resumed weeks later). 

In the library, the user could in theory recreate a transfer from start by calling `setup()` again, but I don't see the use case, as it's as easy to call the constructor.

Your curl request will actually upload a file, but your `requests.put(url, data='/home/<USER>/testfile2.txt')` will make a PUT request with the string `/home/<USER>/testfile2.txt` as the body. Is this a bug?

I see that in your latest log you apparently get a 201 response with Requests, instead of 403. Was something uploaded successfully then?

I don't know your environment, but you could turn off mutual authentication in requests-kerberos, if that's acceptable for your scenario. Maybe curl succeeds if it's not doing that step.

Ah, makes sense.  In that case, your config might look like this:

```
---
actions:
  1:
    action: delete_indices
    description: Delete all indices but the 10 most recent, by creation_date
    options:
      ignore_empty_list: True
      continue_if_exception: False
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: prefix
      value: 'logstash-'
    - filtertype: count
      count: 10
      use_age: True
      source: creation_date
```

Note that `reverse` is omitted.  That's because the default is `True`, so it doesn't need to be there.  Also note the use of single quotes for the pattern filter `value`, `'logstash-'`.  This is because it's used as part of a regular expression.  It's best practices to single-quote anything in the value field.

Here are the testing results from CPython 2.7.13.

```
$ python -m timeit -c "isinstance('abc', str)"
10000000 loops, best of 3: 0.0683 usec per loop
$ python -m timeit -c "type('abc') is str"
10000000 loops, best of 3: 0.0681 usec per loop
$ python -m timeit -c "isinstance(1, str)"
10000000 loops, best of 3: 0.139 usec per loop
$ python -m timeit -c "type(1) is str"
10000000 loops, best of 3: 0.0679 usec per loop
```

So it's much slower for `isinstance()` to fail a type match on an object. This is probably because `int`s have `Number` as their base class. Let's stick to `type()` then.

Also, now we are calling `type(value)` three types. It's better to assign it to a temporary variable and then reuse that variable later.

> This is indeed a very good occasion to also look at the new workflow module, which offers powerful curating possibilities, and which could, together with wider usage of tags and notifications, provide a nice alternative to HGF's use of alerts/baskets for curation purposes.

:+1: Be sure we'll have a very close look. Every line of our code we can get rid of is a good line. If we manage to move this to deposit we'll not stick to our solution.

However, I'd like to keep a few things in mind:
* We have curators, but it's not like on Inspire that they are full time cataloguers.
* We need to keep the learning curve for them at a minimum.
* We can't call them in at once and give them a decent training.
* A lot of, in your book, old fashioned stuff looks to them like rocket science from mars. I learned that even mail filtering is no established procedure in 2014. Let alone tagging. As Morpheus put it: "Welcome to the desert of the real."

Our experience from the trenches is, that predefining basket and alerts that replenish the baskets and remove stuff that's already worked on (automatically that they never see or change anything there) works well and follows the expectations. We even added some functionality to `alertengine` for the removal part. Note that the improvement in 2014 is, that people don't _print_ the revision lists first. IRL it is still a common workflow that publication lists are sent in by email, printed(!) and then keyed in again. (We work on that...) I even saw hand written lists of URLs(!) handed over the desk for review as common procedure.

So, my feeling is that more modern interfaces like tags, queries  etc. ("what 'all people' use in gmail"; no, IRL they don't) will just not work as the learning curve gets to steep. IOW: I'd have to start to explain what a tag _is_ followed by what a _query_ is followed by how to search for a tag and get back the list. Compared to: (link) is your basket work on this till it's empty.

Still be sure we'll definitely check out deposit and workflows and all the other fancy stuff. ;-)

Sidenote: if you change `perform_request_search()` _API_ into something else this will require a lot of work on our end besides migrations. Most likely we'll need to make up some layer here to keep it at a minimum. I'd prefer if this function stays whatever it calls in the backend. But that's another story.

> However, my point still stands. I'm not 100% sure about Kallisto, but other alignment-free quantifiers (thinking of Sailfish, for a sec) are two-pass (Index then Quantify). How do we accommodate those tools? Do we? Do we force them into a single Job?

IMO, if the indexing step has different input requirements than the tool (like in Kallisto's case), they are separate job functions.  This is just an example, because people are going to have opinions on return format (here I'm returning a FileStoreID of a tarball with Kallisto's outputs), and whether or not arguments should always be FileStoreIDs and not something like a URL.  Sometimes it's convenient to roll steps into one functions — like a simple `faidx` if the .fai file isn't present and which doesn't require any other inputs.

```python
def run_kallisto(job, cores, r1_id, r2_id, kallisto_index):
    """
    RNA quantification via Kallisto

    :param JobFunctionWrappingJob job: passed automatically by Toil
    :param int cores: Number of cores to run Kallisto with
    :param str r1_id: FileStoreID of fastq (pair 1)
    :param str r2_id: FileStoreID of fastq (pair 2 if applicable, otherwise pass None for single-end)
    :param str kallisto_index: FileStoreID for Kallisto index file
    :return: FileStoreID from Kallisto output
    :rtype: str
    """
    work_dir = job.fileStore.getLocalTempDir()
    # Retrieve files
    parameters = ['quant',
                  '-i', '/data/kallisto.idx',
                  '-t', str(cores),
                  '-o', '/data/',
                  '-b', '100']
    job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(kallisto_index, os.path.join(work_dir, "kallisto.idx"))
    if r1_id and r2_id:
        job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(r1_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'R1_cutadapt.fastq'))
        job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(r2_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'R2_cutadapt.fastq'))
        parameters.extend(['/data/R1_cutadapt.fastq', '/data/R2_cutadapt.fastq'])
    else:
        job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(r1_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'R1_cutadapt.fastq'))
        parameters.extend(['--single', '-l', '200', '-s', '15', '/data/R1_cutadapt.fastq'])
    # Call: Kallisto
    docker_call(tool='quay.io/ucsc_cgl/kallisto:0.42.4--35ac87df5b21a8e8e8d159f26864ac1e1db8cf86',
                work_dir=work_dir, parameters=parameters)
    # Tar output files together and store in fileStore
    output_files = [os.path.join(work_dir, x) for x in ['run_info.json', 'abundance.tsv', 'abundance.h5']]
    tarball_files(tar_name='kallisto.tar.gz', file_paths=output_files, output_dir=work_dir)
    return job.fileStore.writeGlobalFile(os.path.join(work_dir, 'kallisto.tar.gz'))
```

Ah I see, you're just working with `script` type hooks (one of the few "languages" supported by `repo: local`).  `script` hooks are by and far the _uncommon_ case.  The majority of hooks involve an isolated prefix environment (which is **git cloned** and then given a prefix environment (virtualenv / nodeenv / ruby environment / isolated gopath / etc.) inside `~/.cache/pre-commit`).

For the most part, `local` hooks are also the uncommon case (the rationale again is above and in the documentation), the golden path is really: specify a repository, it's version, and the hookids that you want to use -- the remote repository provides the thinking bits (what files does it apply to? what language is it implemented in? what is the executable? etc.) instead of needing to copy this information around everywhere.

Also, for testing your own hooks you _probably_ want to be testing them in the way that others will use your repository: via `repo: ...`.  This is why `try-repo` is designed to be as close as possible to how one would actually use a repository.  This is why [`pre-commit-hooks` _installs itself_](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/e80813e7e9bceeb263a4329341f2681074fa725a/.pre-commit-config.yaml#L2-L3) -- even why [`pre-commit` installs itself](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/834ed0f229a39c986b241374f6d338632e003b5f/.pre-commit-config.yaml#L15-L18) (a neat bit of history, pre-commit's first hook was actually [itself](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/commit/af7e23aae5eeb2503779efa858e57d7f22120360) -- v. meta).

I hope you understand why I don't want to extend `local` to also perform installation:
- there's already _designed_ ways to do what you want
- `local` has a documented purpose -- and it doesn't match this
- installation / invalidation of installation state would be nearly impossible
- pre-commit will not pollute your working directory with installations (not just because I find it impolite but also because it has [technical problems](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/pull/91) (pre-commit used to do this!))
- pre-commit is designed for _repeatability_, there are no ["floating version installs"](https://pre-commit.com/#using-the-latest-version-for-a-repository).

The `setup.py` of PyAutoGUI [imports itself](https://github.com/asweigart/pyautogui/blob/master/setup.py#L6), which in turn triggers the following code, that eventually creates the error you see:

```python
try:
    import Quartz
except:
    assert False, "You must first install pyobjc-core and pyobjc: https://pyautogui.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html"
```

I think this is a flaw in PyAutoGUI’s packaging. Checking just the first two pages I found three open issues that boil down to this same problem:

* asweigart/pyautogui#184
* asweigart/pyautogui#180
* asweigart/pyautogui#151

I guess the most sensitive way is to let PyAutoGUI fix this. There’s not much Pipenv (and Pip) can do to amend this.

/cc @asweigart

----

Workaround:

* Generate the Pipfile (it will error, but that’s OK)
* Remove PyAutoGUI from Pipfile
* `pipenv install`
* `pipenv install PyAutoGUI` (this will add auto PyAutoGUI to Pipfile and Pipfile.lock)

This should guarantee to work as long as you keep Pipfile.lock, and install from it (i.e. do not run `pipenv install --ignore-lockfile`).

@kazemSafari @fru @GaelReinaudi @11maxed11 @chrisfilo See https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/1018.

The reason TensorBoard samples events from disk and serves that sampled subset to the frontend (of say 10 steps per image tag) is because TensorBoard serves data stored in memory (python data structures), and storing entire log directories (all events) would be too large for memory.

Also, reading from disk to find events at particular steps would be too slow for many cases, especially within distributed file systems.

I think this issue warrants some more thought. We are developing a new backend based on SQL. This lets the user write to for instance SQLite databases, and TensorBoard would (relatively quickly) read from them. However, this effort will launch in probably a few months, and unfortunately, we might end up sampling in the end to conserve database space.

This issue warrants more thought from us. Many have broached it, but we lack a robust solution.

I do not get it. You should be able to start a command as long as you can `ssh worker command` from head node.

So it looks to me like a `Chained_TaskEngine` or `PassThrough_TaskEngine`, basically 

1. You set up a sos task queue from the head node to a point that you can do your work remotely on worker if you are on head node.
2. You set up a task queue on your workstation and remotely control the task queue on the head node. Something like:

```
sos run script -q headnode
```

send task to headnode. This would trigger the following command
```
sos execute task -q worker
```

which sends the task to worker. Because our `sos execute` command can already work with or without `-q` option, the actual implementation should not be very difficult.

I think a reasonable thing to do without referring to your configuration is to 

```
walltime = '6:00:00'
job_template = |
    #PBS walltime=${walltime}
```
so the template is exposed to a dictionary with `walltime`, which becomes the default value if users do not specify `walltime`. Then the value would be overridden with the task option `walltime.

Expected behavior:
1. User submit tasks to task queue through the Jupyter notebook, using either `%set -q pbs` or `task: queue=pbs`.
2. The cell would return after submission of jobs and not wait for the completion of jobs.
3. A number of icons would be displayed to indicate the status of jobs. When all icons turn green, all tasks are completed (and result copied from cluster to local file).

The plan is 

1. Add task engines to the sos kernel. The task engine would be alive throughout the jupyter session.
2. Add the no-wait-for-task mode to interactive executor
3. The executor would receive a list of pending tasks upon exit.
4. The executor would send a list of pending tasks to the frontend through the special communication channel.
5. The task engine would send updates of task status through the communication chennel.
6. The frontend would display a few icons to show the status of the tasks.

We will have to modify the Jupyter kenel ([`kernel.js`](https://github.com/vatlab/SOS/blob/master/sos/jupyter/kernel.js)) for item 5 so I will ping Jame and Jun after 1-5 are completed.

I update @dorodnic 's code.

The streams index as you said is not very intuitive, there is not correlation between index of the stream and the other streams that are streamed from the same sensor.
The way to start streaming are:
1. Using `sensor.open(stream_profile)`
    If you use the first option, you actually only choose the index of the stream from the collection you'll get from `sensor.get_stream_profiles()` - there is no way to create a stream profile yourself. So if you choose: `Infrared #2 (Video Stream: Y8 640x480@ 30Hz)` and `Depth #0 (Video Stream: Z16 640x480@ 30Hz)` you know which profiles you chose.

2. Using `config.enalbe_stream(...+5 overloads)` and `pipeline.start(config)`.
    Using this option you need to specify which index of the stream you want, or pass a wild card.
    If pass a wildcard then we assume you only want 1 steam of that type, and if you want two of the same type, you need to pass `1` and `2`.

In order to stream the depth data only you don't have to apply the patch, only the udev rules.
This will ,however, limit the device capabilities, and to take advantage of certain features the device provides you need the patches applied (see below).
We do not recommend using Librealsense with a live depth camera without patch/dkms applied.

As for the message you receive - please run "realsense-viewer" application and try to stream the depth sensor and report the results to better understand the nature of the error

With regards to the 4.4.0-59 - it provided for the Depth formats (Z16/Y8) to be streamed out of the box without applying patches.
This is sufficient to stream and receive the depth data, but in order to use advanced properties, such as metadata or motion tracking data (where appropriate) you need to apply the additional patches.

@stefanv I was quite confused by the documentation here. While that bit implicitly implies that a list is desired when specifying load_func, the latter example with `imread_convert` uses the normal pattern.

Another source of confusion is the fact that the `_files` attribute is not emptied by `_find_images` when it finds no images. This also means that whenever  any file matched by the pattern is not loaded by PIL, the `_files` attribute goes out of sync with the images in the collection. I doubt that this is intended functionality?

I'm willing to take a shot at fixing this, but I'm not clear on what the best solution is. It seems the possible options are to
 1. Always use `_find_images` to try and count the frames, which will fully disable loading any files not supported by PIL.
 2. Always set `_num_frames` to `len(_files)`, which makes the former parameter redundant, and breaks functionality for multi-image tiffs, etc.
 3. Somehow use the given `load_func` in place of PIL in `_find_images` to read the number of frames, which is probably not possibly without more specifications placed on `load_func`
 4. Keep the current behaviour and document it explicitly (and possibly fix the `_files` desync issue)

Any suggestions / comments?

Just to add a note that I think this amount of verbose info is causing serious usability issues in every day practice (and it's hitting fairly vanilla use patterns beyond developers, as any installation of the base R package will lead to this).

There are really two problems with the current mode:

- the amount of verbosity is enough to become seriously annoying when switching environments frequently.

- it's very easy for all this to drown another relevant message, especially since having this much info come out on every activation is basically training my brain to simply ignore *anything* that gets printed.

Absent a controllable verbosity flag right now, I think a simple compromise solution could be to print a single line that says:

```
Your compiler defaults have changed, for more see: /path/to/info/file.txt
```

A *single* file can be generated on env activation with all this stuff somewhere inside the env. 

I bet this would eliminate the issue for most users, and for anyone who actually needs to read that information, it's actually easier to type

```
grep CPPFLAGS  /path/to/info/file.txt
```

than to scan in terminal output.

I hope this is useful - I can't contribute to conda at this time, but I think the above solution could be implemented relativel easily, without needing the system to support a verbosity flag throughout. I had a look at the activate scripts, and it would be a matter of redirecting their diff output to a file. 

HTH!

@talumbau – The root of the problem is that **conda 4.0.3 cannot download new packages**. You can, however, install packages you've already downloaded and cached. So even if you do ``conda install conda=4.0.2``, if your current installed version is 4.0.3 and you don't have 4.0.2 cached, you will get an error.

What you need to do is to find an older version of conda cached on your system (see @kalefranz's comment above), switch to this first, and then do ``conda update conda`` from there.

Okay, the problem is that `import theano.sandbox.cuda` calls `gpu_init()` without parameters just to check whether CUDA is available at all. It does not set an active device yet, but it includes a call to `cudaGetDeviceCount()`: https://github.com/Theano/Theano/blob/c89d973/theano/sandbox/cuda/cuda_ndarray.cu#L3194
This implicitly creates a CUDA context used for all subsequent CUDA calls. Now when you fork the process afterwards, the child will inherit the CUDA context and try to use it when you call `use("gpu1")`, failing at `cudaGetDeviceProperties` in `gpu_init()`: https://github.com/Theano/Theano/blob/c89d973/theano/sandbox/cuda/cuda_ndarray.cu#L3217
The reason is that a CUDA context cannot be forked.

A solution might be to record the ID of the process that created the context (i.e., before setting `g_gpu_context_active` from 0 to 1), and create and activate a new context at the beginning of `gpu_init()` if we find there already is a context for a different process ID. The functions for managing contexts are in the Driver API: http://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-driver-api/group__CUDA__CTX.html
If you get to a working solution, send a PR to Theano. (This is for the old backend, which is slowly getting phased out, but I think they will still accept it. I don't know if the new backend has the same problem.) Also please report back how things work out!

3) the encapsulation via `ddtrace-run` is not mandatory. You can instrument your libraries using the snippets defined in our documentation. You'll obtain the same result. I agree that eagerly writing `__annotations__` would make things simpler. But being *willing* to write to that attribute doesn't necessarily mean the app *wants* or expects to write to that attribute right now. The code might be something like:

```python
if 'my-key' in IAnnotations(obj):
   # We've done something special with this object before, handle that case
# There is no else, we're not adding the key here. 
```

Or simply:
```python
things = IAnnotations(obj).get('my-key', ())
for thing in things:
   ...
```

One concrete example I have of that in an app I work on has to do with invitation codes. Most users will never have invitations sent to them, but we need to check for each user when a certain page is accessed for that user.

The main benefit of delaying adding `__annotations__`, presumably, has to do with `Persistent` objects, and avoiding extra database writes (the write-on-read scenario, which is often a problem). (For the (rare, but they do exist) apps that do `transaction.abort()` for what are supposed to be side-effect free transactions, such as a HTTP GET request---thus enforcing HTTP semantics---eagerness leads to extra work creating and assigning an attribute that will be thrown away.)

Thanks for the detailed analysis.  I think I agree with you on all of your points.  However I would like to add a few points for you to consider;

1) Openthread currently does not make use of templates.  Adding them here would also add a requirement to all developers that they understand and can read/write template code, thus adding arguable cost to the ongoing maintenance of the Library.

2) For point of reference; a full Openthread build contains, by my count, 26 timers.  Therefore adding 4 bytes to a timer results in 104 bytes of additional system RAM. 

3) Usec resolution is currently only necessary for CSMA backoff, which in turn is only necessary if the radio does not provide builtin retry support.  Future need for additional usec precision is up for debate, but I suspect that it is unlikely.

4) Some platforms may be unable to provide usec resolution.  Currently, the only example configured to provide usec resolution is the Nordic solution. The others have all been made to round up to the next millisecond.

Given those last two points, would you consider that having a usec timer object may not be necessary at all.  Instead a platform could provide a precision alarm API with callback and the CSMAbackoff would rely on that.  Furthermore, if the platform is unable to provide this precision alarm, then it can be configured to use a standard msec timer (this would be equivalent to the current solution for all examples except the Nordic).  Until such a time arrives that we have multiple needs for usec precision timers we would defer implementation of a usec timer.

So there would need to be a build time config param added to configure the backoff logic so that it either relies on the msec backoff timer or the usec platform alarm.  Such configurations exist for other reasons in openthread-core-default-config.h.
I think my latest changes satisfy all your requests. Please let me know if there is anything else you would like changed.

Something like this could potentially go in the [Traitlet events](https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/examples/Widget%20Events.html#Traitlet-events) section of the Widget events documentation. I think that there are a few things that could be improved with the snippet. I would avoid using `exec` as much as possible. Besides being unpythonic, it opens up security issues around code injection (though it doesn't look like this example suffers from them). See [this](https://nedbatchelder.com/blog/201206/eval_really_is_dangerous.html) for a discussion.

As I understand, you are trying to use exec to dispatch the change to the correct list. An alternative would be to pass the list as a second argument to `on_text`:

```py
def on_text_change(change, list_to_change):
    list_to_change.append(change['new'])
```

You can then parametrize `on_change` using lambdas in your call to `observe`:

```py
from ipywidgets import Text, HBox

t1 = Text(continuous_update=False)
t2 = Text(continuous_update=False)
result1, result2 = [], []

t1.observe(lambda change: on_text_change(change, result1), 'value', type='change')
t2.observe(lambda change: on_text_change(change, result2), 'value', type='change')

HBox([t1, t2])
```

As a further improvement, to avoid duplicating code and to make this more robust against possible future scope changes, you could use a loop across the number of widgets you want to make.

```py
from ipywidgets import Text, HBox

NUMBER_WIDGETS = 3

widgets = []
results = []

def instantiate_widget(description):
    widget = Text(description=description, continuous_update=False)
    result = []
    widget.observe(lambda change: on_text_change(change, result), 'value', type='change')
    return widget, result

for iwidget in range(NUMBER_WIDGETS):
    widget, result = instantiate_widget(description=str(iwidget))
    widgets.append(widget)
    results.append(result)

HBox(widgets)
```

Unfortunately 
```python
Money(100, 'USD') + F('money')
```

Will fail instantly with something like that:
```python
tests/test_models.py:42: in <module>
    class TestVanillaMoneyField:
tests/test_models.py:90: in TestVanillaMoneyField
    (Money(100, 'USD') + F('money'), Money(200, 'USD')),
/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/moneyed/classes.py:109: in __add__
    'Money and non-Money instance.')
E   TypeError: Cannot add or subtract a Money and non-Money instance.
```

So, it relies on `Money.__add__` implementation inside `py-moneyed`, but in fact these things should be equal:
```python
Money(100, 'USD') + F('money')
```

```python
F('money') + Money(100, 'USD')
```

I didn't think about the implementation on `django-money` side, but it may be tricky, I'll try to do that.

>  I mean, ideally it would generate this:
> money__amount=100 + money__amount WHERE (...) and money__currency='USD'
> ...but that's not possible.
> So raising an exception is perhaps the best? I.e. "unsupported operation, cannot add known currency to unknown currency blah"?

Such checks are implemented [here](https://github.com/django-money/django-money/pull/169/files#diff-a3ba76a61c67ec38bfe7ac546e2548e0R164)

About permitted operations

Preliminary I've decided to disallow following operations:
1. Any operations on money instances with different currencies. This one seems OK to me
2. Exponentiation
3. Add or subtract money with not-money
4. Multiplication, division, modulo with money instances on both sides of expression

In 2-4 I rely on `py-moneyed` `Money` implementation, where these actions are disallowed. But in Django we have decimal field with value, and these value can be used in all arithmetic operations. So, if someone wants to deal with decimal part of `MoneyField` these operations will fail, which may cause some inconvenience for users. And we have to decide here about expected behavior. May be the most expected behavior here is to allow users to deal with decimal part of `MoneyField` without restrictions on arithmetic operations. What do you think?

I found the content returned by the request was empty because the server was returning a 400 error, so lxml had nothing to parse. I discovered SharePoint is expecting a base64 array. After a lot of trial and error I found the following code works with a string, a text file and binary files. 

 ```
def add(self, filename=None, content=None, upload_file=None):
        if upload_file:
            if not filename:
                filename = upload_file
            with open(upload_file, "rb") as filein:
                f = filein.read()
                econtent = base64.b64encode(f).decode()
        else:
            econtent = base64.encodebytes(content.encode()).decode()
        xml = SP.AddAttachment(SP.listName(self.list_id),
                               SP.listItemID(str(self.row_id)),
                               SP.fileName(filename),
                               SP.attachment(econtent))
        response = self.opener.post_soap(LIST_WEBSERVICE,
                                         xml,
                                         soapaction='http://schemas.microsoft.com/sharepoint/soap/AddAttachment')
```

> For a tool (importer or exporter) that consciously checks the op. version and op. name (using the appropriate indirection, if we have it), the indirection presumably makes no difference.

I am not sure what you mean by "no difference" here; the importer definitely has to know about the indirection, otherwise it cannot correctly select an implementation for an `op_type`.

> We have an interesting effect if an exporter exports an operator-name (say "Slice") and this code is not made robust to versioning changes: that is, if it exports a newer op-version without changing the name "Slice", then the name "Slice" acquires a different (newer) semantics.

Yes, this is a good point. If an exporter wants to "upgrade" the operator version they are exporting, they're responsible for auditing every BC breaking change from their old version to the new one, and fixing any of their other exports to be conformant with any new definitions. This seems like a good thing to me, but perhaps there are non-obvious second order effects.

Per the PEP, zipapps are zip files with a shebang at the top and a `__main__.py` file inside of them. This is the "magic" that allows the Python interpreter to be able to execute them. If the interpreter itself were included in the zipapp, it would no longer _be_ a zipapp and would require some other mechanism to execute.

I believe there *are* some tools that ship the interpreter as well, pyinstaller comes to mind 🙂

Sure, I know this -  and unfortunately that's what I'm doing, because I've no other choice -
and it's not only in the deployment scripts - it's also in every development environment that relies on the prometheus client.

In my humble opinion relying solely on environment variables in general, and specifically for dynamically linking types - as done in the core.py module, shown below - is problematic:

`if 'prometheus_multiproc_dir' in os.environ:
    _ValueClass = _MultiProcessValue()
else:
    _ValueClass = _MutexValue`

This dependency is even worse when it's in an external library that your code depends upon - because it forces the dependent code to declare environment variables that are not even related to its domain.

Think about this: what if you had to define even a single environment variable for each and every library your code depends on - you would end up with a gazillion environment variables ;)

I believe that relying on some global initialization code is definitely the way to go.

Again, this is my humble opinion - and if you disagree, then it's fine.
On the other hand, if what I've written makes sense to you - then I would be happy to try and give a few tips about how I think this can be resolved.

I actually wrote a small PR just for that. I wanted to support virtual envs in my Hadoop jobs. 
You have to extend the HadoopJobRunner and the JobTask class. But it is not hard.
 
In this post I describe how I did it: http://mfcabrera.com/python/2016/04/25/python-streaming-blog-org.html 

But in a nutshell:

```python
class VenvHadoopJobRunner(HadoopJobRunner):
	def __init__(self, **kwargs):

		config = configuration.get_config()
		streaming_jar = config.get('hadoop', 'streaming-jar')
		super(VenvHadoopJobRunner, self).__init__(
			streaming_jar=streaming_jar,
			**kwargs
```

Then in your `job_runner` method :
```python
	def job_runner(self):

		config = configuration.get_config()
		venv_path = self.venv_path
		# (...) 
		return VenvHadoopJobRunner(
			archives=[venv_archive],
		)

```
