What is the problem you are seeing? Your fix seems to avoid trapping KI (by trapping Ex instead of BaseEx) and then to avoid `out_queue.put()` on KI, so I don't see how the deadlock can be related to the put which is now skipped. Is KI happening *during* `out_queue.put()`? If so, it seems like your test does not exercise that.

Try this in your test: Put a long sleep (but not too long, else you'll have trouble killing the procs, as I mention below -- maybe 5seconds) in front of queue.get(). Then, run only the failing test. (I have no idea how to do that with tox, but you can do it with `nosetests --no-skip test/worker_test.py:KeyboardInterruptBehaviorTest.test_propagating_with_par_scheduling`. With that, Ctrl-C should still work! But it doesn't. Instead, the sub-processes keep running after nose has quit, another worker dying every few seconds, which is very annoying. This looks like an architectural problem, or at least a mistake in how you are using nose. I don't have time to dive deep into your code. But you could probably ameliorate the problem by using timeouts for all blocking calls, in a loop. (Both put and get, I think.) As a general principle, nothing should block forever.

If the problem is only for users who have disabled GC, maybe use a try/finally somewhere to close the queue in all workers. But that won't solve the problem I've (maybe) found by sleeping ahead of get().

I think you can force the problem to go away by sending a specific signal to sub-procs. SIGKILL, I guess.

# Project dependancies

This a list of all of the dependancies the CMS currently has and my evaluation of whether it is required.

### django

Required.

### psycopg2

Required.

### django-suit

Not required. When we redesign the admin this can be removed.

### Pillow

Required for image manipulation.

### django-reversion

Required to allow users to rollback their changes. Needs to be used more in custom models.

### django-historylinks

A useful tool for maintaining historical links. Could potentially be brought into the core of the CMS.

### django-watson

Provides a great search backend, we use it well.

### django-extensions

Only used during development, but a useful thing to have.

### Werkzeug

Also only used during development, but useful to have.

### raven

Provides our Sentry integration. This may be phased out in favour of Opbeat.

### django-compressor

Used to minify our CSS and JS. This could be done with Gulp.

### sorl-thumbnail

Used for thumbnailing, useful to have.

### bcrypt

Used for improving password security, required.

### onespacemedia-server-management

Used for deploying and managing servers. Could potentially be brought into the project, but works well as an external dependancy.  It's dependancies are reliant on Python 2 is limiting for the CMS as a whole.

### requests

Used to bring in images from a remote source for the Adobe SDK. Could potentially be replaced with urllib2.

### python-social-auth

Used to allow Onespacemedia staff to log in automatically. Has it's own problems (particulary around losing names and the amount of effort required for setup). Could potentially be brought into the project.

### django-cachalot

Used for simple caching of queries, makes sites faster with no code required.

### python-memcached

Used in conjunction with Cachalot primarily, but other services can make use of it too.

### geoip

Used to Geolocate users for the regionalisation work. Could be removed upon a rework of the languages system if it were to be based on browser headers or similar.

I just pushed https://github.com/wez/watchman/tree/win with my hacking in progress; it's been sitting on my laptop for a while and I just rebased on master as of today and make sure that it still builds on both osx and windows.

You will need the microsoft visual C++ compiler to build it; you can use the community edition for this.
The compiler tools (`cl`, `nmake` and so on) must be in your path.
To run the integration tests you will need arcanist and php installed and in your path.

To build and test:

```
cd watchman
nmake -f winbuild\Makefile check
arc test
```

The integration tests are a bit rough and ready largely because of the filesystem semantics on windows are not POSIX; there are a lot of errors around attempting to delete dirs while handles are still open on them.  There are also some errors with `/` instead of `\` in path names.  These are for the most part cosmetic.

The current status is `26 of 74 tests failed`.

If you're interested in helping with hacking and testing, you are most welcome!  We're at the cleaning things up stage.

If you're interested in making a client connect to it, you can connect to a named pipe for the current user; for example, my watchman instance listens at `\\.\pipe\watchman-wez`.  The code to select the default name of the pipe can be found here: https://github.com/wez/watchman/blob/win/main.c#L456

Once connected, you use the standard JSON or BSER watchman protocol to communicate with the server.

How does this work? Looks like most modifications are in tests/

Using below snippet:
```
import mxnet as mx

ctx = mx.cpu(0)
data = mx.nd.zeros((1,3,224,224), ctx=ctx, dtype='float64')
net_fp32 = mx.gluon.model_zoo.vision.resnet34_v2(pretrained=True, ctx=ctx)
net_fp32.cast('float64')
net_fp32.hybridize()
pred = net_fp32.forward(data)
net_fp32.export('resnet34_fp16', 0)
print('export fp16 model')

sm = mx.sym.load('resnet34_fp16-symbol.json')
inputs = mx.sym.var('data', dtype='float64')
net_fp16 = mx.gluon.SymbolBlock(sm, inputs)
net_fp16.collect_params().load('resnet34_fp16-0000.params', ctx)
pred = net_fp16.forward(data)
```

Below are my findings:
1. Casting worked fine.
2. Saved parameters are in the correct format (fp64 in my sample code)
3. sym.load worked fine. If I infer symbol's type (sym.infer_type(data='float64') I get correct inferred type (float64) for all params.

Below is the issue:
1. When you create mx.gluon.SymbolBlock(sm, input). It creates initializes parameters in the Block. By default, this uses Float32.
See here - https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/gluon/block.py#L1058 and the behavior is "If there is not parameter to get, it creates one and uses default type (fp32) https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/gluon/parameter.py#L688

I am working on the fix.

@szha I think this change causes a regression reported in [issue #11772](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/11772).

The variable number of batches per epoch is kind of intentional. What happens here is basically: if the number of images is not divisible by the batch size, then iterator needs to fill the last batch with something after it used all of the images. By default it uses images from the next epoch (you can change that behavior by setting `round_batch` option of the iterator to `False`). Then, during the next epoch, it does not use those images (otherwise those images would be overrepresented in the training process), which in some cases may result in that epoch having 1 less batch.

I wanted to add important notes following a conversation with Tao.

So our security model currently lives in FAB, and trying to reference it in Airflow core (either through the FAB's model) or `AirflowSecurityManager` (build on top of FAB's `SecurityManager`) requires importing / instantiating the Flask app. We absolutely do not want this. 

So there are two possible paths to avoid this dependency:

1. One is to say that access control lives on the web server and anything that needs access control would be done through REST. That means that if we want access control in the CLI (and we do!), we would have to re-write the CLI to go through the REST api. We clearly want to do this at some point anyway.

2. Promote the access control as an Airflow-core concept. This requires refactoring access control logic out of `www_rbac` and into the core of Airflow itself. To do this, could "stub" the security models from FAB into `airflow/models.py`, meaning we can access permission information without loading up the Flask App. **I'm a bit confused as I couldn't find the rbac-related migrations in the repo**. This also means refactoring things that are now in `AirflowSecurityManager` as a construct that does not depend on FAB's `SecurityManager`.
Please make sure to document this in UPDATING.md as you are introducing backward incompatible changes.
